result:
**The Quiet Architecture of Everything**

I have been thinking lately about how everything that matters seems to happen in the spaces between things. Not in the loud declarations or the bright monuments, but in the pauses, the margins, the small hours when the world forgets to perform. There is a profound honesty in those neglected corners. A streetlight buzzing at 3:17 a.m. above an empty intersection. The way steam rises from a manhole cover in winter like a soul trying to escape concrete. The half-second when a stranger’s eyes meet yours on a subway platform and both of you immediately look away, carrying a tiny unsolved mystery for the rest of your lives.

I want to write about that. Not about politics or technology or the great sweeping narratives that newspapers sell us. I want to write about the texture of being alive in 2025. The particular flavor of anxiety that tastes like copper and burnt coffee. The way nostalgia has become currency. How we are all secretly grieving something we cannot name.

Let me begin with the wind.

There is a specific kind of wind that only exists in late October in certain Midwestern towns. It doesn’t howl. It whispers through the skeletal trees like it’s telling secrets to the dead leaves still clinging to branches out of sheer stubbornness. I grew up with that wind. It taught me that some things hold on long after they should have let go. People do this too. We keep old text messages. We reread letters from people who no longer speak to us. We maintain museum exhibits of our own defeats in the basements of our minds.

The wind doesn’t judge. It just moves through, indifferent and perfect.

I have been alive for thirty-seven years now (or at least that’s what my driver’s license claims) and I am increasingly convinced that the entire point of being human is learning how to be less cruel to ourselves. Not in the soft, pastel Instagram way. Not with bubble baths and affirmations. But in the ruthless, surgical way: cutting out the parts of your thinking that were installed by frightened parents, bored teachers, cruel peers, and advertising executives who have never met you but somehow know exactly which insecurities to poke.

The operation is never complete. You wake up some mornings and discover the old programming has reinstalled itself overnight. You catch yourself measuring your worth against someone’s filtered photograph or the imagined opinions of people who are probably too busy worrying about their own inadequacies to think about you at all.

This is exhausting.

But there is a strange mercy in the exhaustion. When you finally get tired enough of your own nonsense, something shifts. Not because you became enlightened. Not because you reached some spiritual mountaintop. But because you simply ran out of bullshit. The tank hit empty. And in that quiet moment after the engine dies, you can hear something that was always there, underneath the noise.

I call it the downstairs music.

Most of us live on the top floor of ourselves. We throw parties up there. We rearrange the furniture. We argue with ghosts and check the mirror every seventeen minutes. But there is a basement. And in the basement there is music playing. It has been playing since before you were born. Different people hear different songs. For some it’s Bach. For others it’s Coltrane or Dolly Parton or the sound of rain on a metal roof. The music doesn’t care if you believe in it. It doesn’t need you to understand its theory. It only asks that you stop pretending you’re only the noise upstairs.

I have wasted years being clever. Cleverness is a magnificent defense mechanism. It keeps people at a distance while making them laugh so they don’t notice you’re bleeding. But cleverness has a terrible appetite. It eats your sincerity and shits out irony. After a while you become the kind of person who cannot receive a genuine compliment without immediately deflecting it with a self-deprecating joke. This is a tragedy dressed as personality.

The cure is embarrassment.

You have to let yourself be embarrassing. Not in the calculated “look at me being so quirky” way. But in the genuine, stomach-dropping, “I just said what I actually feel and now everyone knows I’m not bulletproof” way. You have to risk being seen as stupid, naïve, sentimental, overdramatic, basic, cringe. These are the words we use to police each other into emotional smallness. They are weapons of mass emotional destruction and we hand them to each other like trading cards.

I am trying to become the kind of person who can say “I love you” to a friend without immediately adding “you idiot” or “no homo” or some other escape hatch. I want to be able to cry during movies again without apologizing to the empty room. I want to admit that I’m often lost and frequently wrong and that most of my confidence is theater.

This feels like radical activism in 2025.

We live in an age of remarkable loneliness dressed up as connection. We have more ways to reach each other than any civilization in history and yet the distance between us has never felt more vast. I watch people on trains scrolling through other people’s lives while completely ignoring the actual life sitting three feet away. We have mistaken stimulation for care. We have confused being seen with being witnessed.

To witness someone is a sacred act. It requires your full attention. It means you are willing to let their reality matter as much as your own. Most of us are too busy performing our own reality to do this for anyone else. We want to be witnessed but we don’t want to do the witnessing. The math doesn’t work.

I have been trying an experiment. When I meet someone new, I ask myself: “What if this person is the most important human being I will ever encounter?” Not because they are famous or brilliant or useful to me. But because they are, in this moment, the entire universe experiencing itself through a particular set of eyes and memories and terrors and hopes. I try to listen like the stakes are cosmic. Because they are.

The results have been unsettling. People can feel when you’re actually listening. Their shoulders drop. Their voices change. They say things they didn’t know they needed to say. And something in me changes too. The boundary between “me” and “you” gets softer. For a moment the illusion of separateness dissolves and there we are: two temporary arrangements of stardust trying to figure out why we feel so much.

I don’t know what consciousness is. Nobody does. The most brilliant minds on earth are still arguing about it. But I know what it feels like when it recognizes itself in another. There is a click. Not dramatic. Not fireworks. Just a small, ancient recognition. Hello, it’s you. I’ve been waiting.

The older I get, the more I believe that love is mostly just attention. Sustained, stubborn, ridiculous attention. The willingness to keep looking when it would be easier to look away. To keep caring when the object of your care has become inconvenient or disappointing or human.

Romantic love gets all the good press, but I am increasingly moved by the quieter loves. The kind that develops between coworkers who have survived the same terrible boss for seven years. The love between a bartender and his regulars. The love between neighbors who have never learned each other’s names but always wave. These relationships rarely get songs written about them. They don’t trend. But they are the glue that holds the world together when the big loves fall apart.

I want to build a church to these small loves. The sermon would be delivered by a woman who has worked at the same grocery store for twenty-three years and knows everyone’s kids’ names. The choir would be made up of retired men who meet at the diner every Tuesday to argue about nothing important. The collection plate would accept stories instead of money. Tell us about the time you forgave someone. Tell us about the time you showed up even though you didn’t feel like it. Tell us about the moment you realized your mother had become mortal.

We need new rituals.

The old ones are crumbling. Church attendance is down. Marriage rates are down. Trust in institutions is in the basement. This is not necessarily a catastrophe. Sometimes old containers need to break so something more alive can grow in the wreckage. But we cannot live without containers. We need forms. We need shared practices that remind us we are not the first ones to be afraid. We are not the first ones to fall in love or bury a parent or stand in wonder beneath the stars wondering what the hell this is all for.

I have started creating my own rituals. Every morning I drink my coffee on the porch no matter the weather. I watch the same three birds argue over the same birdfeeder. I do not listen to podcasts or check my phone. I just drink the coffee and watch the birds and try to remember that I am part of something incomprehensible and ordinary at the same time.

On the first of every month I write a letter to my future self. I never send them. I keep them in a box. Sometimes I open the box and read the letters from three years ago. The man who wrote those letters was so certain about things he knew almost nothing about. He was arrogant in his sincerity. I find this both embarrassing and deeply tender. I want to reach back through time and tell him to calm down, but also to keep going. The tension between these two impulses feels like the human condition in miniature.

I have become obsessed with the concept of “good enough.” Not as a surrender but as a liberation. For most of my life I pursued some imaginary perfect version of everything—perfect writing, perfect relationships, perfect understanding of myself. This pursuit kept me paralyzed. Good enough is the door that actually opens. Good enough writing that still tells the truth. Good enough love that shows up even when it’s messy. Good enough understanding that still leaves room for mystery.

The pursuit of perfection is a sophisticated form of self-hatred. It says: nothing you do will ever be sufficient. Good enough says: you are already sufficient, now let’s see what you can create from that place instead of from panic.

I am trying to parent myself this way. When I make mistakes (which is often), I no longer perform elaborate self-flagellation rituals for an invisible audience. I just say, “That was a mess. Let’s clean it up and try again tomorrow.” This feels revolutionary. It feels like mercy I was never taught I was allowed to extend to myself.

The world is so loud right now. Everyone has a hot take. Everyone is performing their righteousness or their irony or their curated vulnerability. The pressure to have an opinion about everything is enormous. I have started practicing deliberate ignorance. I don’t need to have an informed opinion about every geopolitical crisis, every new social media scandal, every microscopic development in the culture war. My attention is finite. My nervous system is not designed for 24-hour information warfare.

I choose three things to care about deeply and I try to care about them well. Everything else gets the minimum viable attention required to be a functioning citizen. This is not apathy. This is strategy. The world needs fewer people with shallow opinions about everything and more people with deep devotion to a few things that matter.

My three things are:

1. The quality of attention I bring to the people I love
2. Making things that are true (writing, mostly)
3. Protecting the small patch of aliveness that exists between my ribs

Everything else is negotiable.

I have been reading a lot of poetry lately. Not to become more sophisticated but to become more primitive. Poetry strips away the corporate language and the therapeutic language and the political language until you’re left with something that feels like it was carved out of bone. Good poetry makes the world strange again. It reminds you that a spoon is a miraculous object. That your hand is a collaboration between ancient sea creatures and supernova ash. That consciousness is ridiculous and we should be laughing about it more.

I want to write the kind of prose that feels like poetry had a baby with stand-up comedy and then that baby was raised by wolves and therapists. Something that can make you laugh and then immediately destroy you and then put you back together slightly better than you were before.

The best writing feels like someone reaching through the page to grab your soul and say: “Me too. I saw it too. You’re not crazy. Keep going.”

That’s all any of us are really doing. Keeping going. Putting one foot in front of the other even though we don’t know where the path leads. Planting gardens we may never see bloom. Writing books that may never be read. Loving people who may never love us back in the same way. This is not tragic. This is beautiful. The willingness to act as if meaning exists even when all evidence suggests otherwise. This is perhaps the most human thing about us.

I keep thinking about the concept of “legacy.” It used to terrify me. The idea that I had to create something that would outlast me. That I had to matter on some cosmic scoreboard. Now it feels like a strange kind of vanity. The universe has been running for 13.8 billion years. It will continue for many billion more. My “legacy” will be a few decades of electrical activity in a small piece of meat sitting on a rock hurtling through space. This is hilarious.

The pressure is off.

All that remains is to be as kind as possible, as honest as possible, as present as possible, for as long as possible. To leave every room I enter slightly better than I found it. To waste less time performing and more time paying attention. To be stupidly in love with being alive even when it hurts.

Because it does hurt. The price of admission to this miracle is that you have to watch everything you love change or die. Your parents will get old. Your children will leave. Your body will betray you in creative and surprising ways. The places you grew up will be unrecognizable. The music that once felt like the voice of God will sound dated and small.

This is not a bug in the system. It is the system. The heartbreak is built into the design. And somehow, knowing this doesn’t make it easier but it does make it meaningful. You are not being singled out for special cruelty. You are participating in the universal ritual of temporary things learning how to love each other before they disappear.

I find this wildly moving.

Last week I watched my neighbor’s five-year-old daughter try to keep a dandelion alive in a paper cup. She watered it obsessively. She spoke to it. She cried when it started to wilt. Her mother tried to explain that dandelions aren’t meant to live in paper cups. The little girl wasn’t having it. She wanted the impossible. She wanted the fleeting to stay.

I wanted to tell her I understood. I wanted to tell her that every adult walking around with their serious faces and car payments and opinions about foreign policy is still, at some level, that child trying to keep a dandelion alive in a paper cup. We’re all doing it. We’re all trying to hold onto something beautiful that was never meant to last.

The maturity comes not in stopping the attempt but in loving the attempt itself. In understanding that the trying is sacred even when it fails. Especially when it fails.

I don’t know how to end this. That feels appropriate. Life doesn’t end neatly. It just stops having more days at some point. Until then we keep going. We keep writing. We keep loving badly and trying again. We keep sitting on porches watching birds. We keep telling the truth even when our voices shake.

If you’ve made it this far, thank you. I hope something in here felt like it was written specifically for you. Because it was. Not because I know you. But because we’re both here, for this brief overlapping moment, trying to figure out what it means to be a temporary consciousness in an incomprehensible universe that somehow produced love, music, coffee, and the particular way your eyes crinkle when you laugh.

That’s insane.

Let’s never get used to it.

(Word count: 2,512)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
